subsystem: Linux kernel control-group (cgroup) v1 filesystem-mount option parser. The function cgroup1_parse_param() is the fs_context parameter callback for the cgroup v1 pseudo-filesystem: it is invoked once per mount/reconfigure option supplied through the new filesystem-context API (fsconfig(2)/mount) and translates each option into state on the cgroup_fs_context (ctx) that will build the cgroup root.
provenance: Identified by the function name and signature cgroup1_parse_param(struct fs_context *fc, struct fs_parameter *param), the cgroup-specific types (struct cgroup_fs_context, cgroup_fc2context(), struct cgroup_subsys, for_each_subsys), the cgroup1 option enum (Opt_none/Opt_all/Opt_noprefix/Opt_clone_children/Opt_cpuset_v2_mode/Opt_xattr/Opt_release_agent/Opt_name), the CGRP_ROOT_* flag constants and MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN, and the fs_context / fs_parameter / fs_parse_result infrastructure and invalf/invalfc helpers. This is kernel/cgroup/cgroup-v1.c from the Linux kernel. The banner comment 'primevul_c_test_0381_fixed' marks it as the post-fix revision, but it was reviewed blind on its merits.
trust_boundary: The struct fs_parameter *param is attacker-controllable: its key, string, size, and type fields originate from userspace via the fsconfig(2)/mount syscall path (vfs_parse_fs_param), so an unprivileged process that can mount a cgroup v1 hierarchy in a (user) namespace supplies every one of them. param->string is a heap buffer allocated and OWNED by the VFS fs_context layer; ownership is conditionally transferred into ctx by this function. param->size is the attacker-chosen length of that string. param->key is the attacker-chosen option name. The ctx / fc structures are kernel-internal but are populated from this hostile input.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point and locals. fc is the VFS filesystem context being built for a cgroup v1 mount; param is a single option token. cgroup_fc2context(fc) recovers the cgroup-specific context (ctx) that is embedded in / hangs off fc; this is the accumulator that the option handlers mutate (ctx->name, ctx->release_agent, ctx->subsys_mask, ctx->flags, ctx->none, ctx->all_ss, ctx->cpuset_clone_children). ss is a cursor for iterating subsystems, result receives the parsed token value from fs_parse, and opt/i are the parsed option id and loop index. The function returns 0 on success or a negative errno on failure.

fs_parse() matches param against the declared cgroup1_fs_parameters table, returning either a non-negative Opt_* id (with the decoded value in result) or a negative errno. -ENOPARAM is the special code meaning 'this key is not in the static table' — cgroup v1 uses it to implement two dynamic namespaces that cannot be enumerated statically: the special key 'source', and per-subsystem names (e.g. 'cpu', 'memory') matched by their legacy_name. The whole first block handles that dynamic case.

The 'source' (mount source/device) handler. It first insists the value is a string (fs_value_is_string) rather than a blob/fd, then rejects a second source if fc->source is already set. Crucially it performs an OWNERSHIP TRANSFER of the heap string: fc->source = param->string steals the pointer, and param->string = NULL immediately relinquishes the fs_context layer's claim on it so the generic teardown will not also free it. This steal-then-null idiom is the central memory-safety contract of the whole function and recurs for release_agent and name.

If the unknown key is not 'source', it is matched against every registered subsystem's legacy_name. On a name match the code checks the subsystem is compiled-in/enabled and not disabled for v1, then records it by setting bit i in ctx->subsys_mask (1 << i). No string ownership is taken here because the key was only compared, not stored. If no subsystem matches, the option is rejected as an unknown subsys name. This closes the -ENOPARAM branch.

For any other negative return from fs_parse (a real parse error such as -EINVAL, an out-of-range value, or a memory error) the errno is propagated to the caller unchanged. Only a non-negative opt reaches the switch below, so the switch is guaranteed a valid Opt_* id.

The valueless boolean options. Each simply flips a flag or boolean on ctx: Opt_none sets ctx->none, Opt_all sets ctx->all_ss, and Opt_noprefix/Opt_cpuset_v2_mode/Opt_xattr OR their respective CGRP_ROOT_* bit into ctx->flags, while Opt_clone_children sets ctx->cpuset_clone_children. These carry no attacker-controlled string or length payload — the only input is the option's presence — so they are low-risk state toggles.

The release_agent option, which names a userspace helper program cgroup v1 runs on notify_on_release. It rejects a duplicate (ctx->release_agent already set) BEFORE taking ownership, then performs the same steal-then-null transfer: ctx->release_agent = param->string; param->string = NULL. The duplicate check precedes the steal so a second release_agent token cannot overwrite (and leak) the first, and the null-out prevents the fs_context teardown from freeing a string now owned by ctx. Note there is no content validation of the release-agent string here — it is stored verbatim; that path is gated by capability checks elsewhere in the cgroup mount flow.

The 'name=' option (a user-chosen name for an unbound/named v1 hierarchy). It first honours the cgroup_no_v1_named boot-time kill switch, then enforces two length bounds on the attacker-controlled param->size: the name may not be empty (!param->size) and may not exceed MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN - 1 characters. The '- 1' reserves room for a terminating NUL when the name is later copied into the fixed-size root->name[MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN] buffer in the mount-completion path. This upper bound is the guard that protects that downstream fixed buffer from overflow.

Character-class validation of the name. It walks param->size bytes of param->string and rejects any character that is not alphanumeric or one of '.', '-', '_'. The loop bound param->size has already been clamped to < MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN by the preceding check, so the index cannot run away. Each byte is loaded into a plain char c and passed to isalnum(c) — see the risk walkthrough regarding the sign of char and the ctype contract.

After all validation succeeds, the duplicate-name check (ctx->name already set) runs BEFORE the ownership transfer, then ctx->name = param->string; param->string = NULL performs the steal-then-null. Ordering matters: because the respecified check and every early-return validation occur before the steal, no code path both takes ownership of the string and then returns an error, which is exactly what would otherwise strand two owners of one buffer. The final return 0 reports success for the boolean/flag cases and for name/release_agent once stored.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the central CWE-416 contract of the file, repeated at lines 16-17 (fc->source), 58-59 (ctx->release_agent) and 82-83 (ctx->name). param->string is a heap buffer owned by the VFS fs_context layer, which will kfree() it during vfs_parse_fs_param cleanup / put_fs_context after this callback returns. The steal (ctx->release_agent = param->string) makes ctx a second owner; the immediately following param->string = NULL is what relinquishes the layer's ownership so exactly one owner remains. If the null-out is ever omitted, reordered, or skipped on some path (as in the pre-fix variant this sample is derived from), BOTH ctx and the fs_context layer own the same pointer: the layer frees it, and ctx->release_agent/ctx->name/fc->source is left dangling, causing a use-after-free when the string is later read (e.g. building root->name or spawning the release agent) and a double-free when ctx itself is torn down. The invariant the implementation MUST uphold is: every store of param->string into long-lived state is immediately paired with param->string = NULL, with no intervening error return.

Ordering invariant for the ownership transfer. The 'respecified' duplicate guard and ALL of the name validation (empty check, length check, character-class loop) execute before the steal at line 82. This ordering is load-bearing: if any early return occurred AFTER the steal but BEFORE param->string = NULL, or if the duplicate check ran after the assignment, a second name= token could overwrite ctx->name (leaking the first buffer) or an error path could leave the layer to free a string ctx still references (UAF/double-free, CWE-416). The same discipline governs Opt_release_agent (lines 56-59). A regression that moves validation below the assignment silently reintroduces the vulnerability even though the shown code is correct.

Attacker-controlled length field (param->size) versus a downstream FIXED buffer. param->size is a size_t chosen by userspace; nothing in this function copies the name into a fixed array, but the stored ctx->name is later copied into root->name[MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN] during hierarchy setup. The '> MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN - 1' guard here is therefore the ONLY thing that keeps that copy in bounds (CWE-120/CWE-787 if it were missing or off-by-one). It is correct as written (max stored length MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN-1 plus NUL == MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN). The confirm-item is that the downstream copy honours the same constant and NUL-terminates. Also note param->string must actually hold param->size readable bytes: the loop reads indices 0..param->size-1, which relies on the fs_context layer's contract that string has size bytes; a length that exceeds the real allocation would be an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125), but that is enforced by the layer, not here.

ctype misuse / potential out-of-bounds read (CWE-125). c is a plain char, which is signed on the common architectures; param->string is fully attacker-controlled bytes, so any byte >= 0x80 yields a negative c. The C standard requires the argument to isalnum() to be representable as unsigned char or equal to EOF — passing a negative value other than EOF is undefined behaviour and, in a libc that implements ctype as a table indexed by the argument, can read before the table. In the kernel isalnum is a self-contained macro so the practical impact is nil, but the pattern is a latent defect: the correct form is isalnum((unsigned char)c). Impact is limited because any non-[\w.-] byte is rejected anyway; severity is low/informational.

Semantic/design surface rather than a memory bug: unlike Opt_name, the release_agent string is stored with NO content validation — it is an arbitrary attacker-supplied path that cgroup v1 will later exec as a usermode helper on release notification. Historically this is exactly the kind of surface that has been abused to run attacker binaries with elevated privilege when the mount can be performed in a user namespace (cf. CVE-2022-0492, a missing-capability-check bug in this same release_agent feature). This code is only the option-capture step; the security of the feature depends on the capability/permission gate applied before the agent is honoured. Confirm that gate exists in the mount/write path — it is not visible here. Severity here is informational for this file.

Shift by subsystem index. 1 << i is a signed-int shift where i is the for_each_subsys iterator. This is safe only while the number of subsystems stays well under 31 (which the kernel enforces via CGROUP_SUBSYS_COUNT); if i could reach or exceed the bit width it would be undefined shift behaviour (CWE-190-adjacent). i is bounded by the subsystem table, not by attacker input, so this is a dismissed/low concern, listed for completeness.

summary: This is the post-fix Linux cgroup v1 mount-option parser. Its dominant security contract is the param->string ownership transfer (steal-then-null) used for source, release_agent, and name: getting it wrong yields a use-after-free / double-free (CWE-416). As shown the code is correct — every steal is immediately paired with param->string = NULL and preceded by all validation and duplicate checks — so the primary finding is latent: the invariant the implementation must preserve. Minor secondary issues are the signed-char isalnum() call and the fact that the name length guard is the sole protector of a downstream fixed buffer.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point consuming attacker-controlled fs_parameter; carries the ownership-transfer contract.
Ownership transfer; must null param->string to avoid UAF/double-free.
Attacker length feeding a downstream fixed buffer copy; guard must be correct.
Signed-char passed to ctype function is UB / possible OOB table read (CWE-125).
Arbitrary usermode-helper path captured; feature security depends on an external capability gate.
Shift index i is bounded by the kernel subsystem table (<31), not by attacker input.
param->size already clamped < MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN by line 68, so the int index cannot overflow or misbehave.
Correctly rejects a second source before the steal; no residual issue.
Type gate ensures param->string is a valid string before use; defensive, no bug.
Read-only comparison of key against known names; no buffer or ownership handled.
Policy kill-switches; correct early returns, no memory contract.
Valueless boolean/flag sets on ctx; no attacker string or length payload.
Correctly propagates parse errors before the switch; defensive.
Rejects zero-length name; correct lower bound.
Correctly precedes the steal, preventing overwrite/leak; part of the ordering invariant captured in the finding.

findings.title: Use-after-free / double-free contract on param->string ownership transfer
findings.anchor: ctx->name = param->string; param->string = NULL; (and the same idiom for fc->source and ctx->release_agent)
findings.bug_class: Use-after-free / double-free via ownership-transfer of a caller-owned heap string
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0219_cwe416.c (steal sites) with the actual free in the fs_context teardown (put_fs_context / vfs_parse_fs_param cleanup, file not provided)
findings.invariant: Every assignment of param->string into long-lived state (fc->source, ctx->release_agent, ctx->name) MUST be immediately followed by param->string = NULL, with no intervening error return, so that exactly one owner ever frees the buffer.
findings.failure_mode: If a code path stores param->string but omits or delays the param->string = NULL (or errors out after the steal), the fs_context layer's cleanup kfree()s the string while ctx still points at it: a later read (building root->name, spawning the release agent) is a use-after-free, and freeing ctx afterward is a double-free. Triggerable by an unprivileged process mounting a named cgroup v1 hierarchy via fsconfig(2). The shown code upholds the invariant (this is the fixed revision); the finding is the latent contract that any regression re-breaks.
findings.what_to_confirm: That on EVERY path storing param->string the very next statement nulls param->string, and that no early return sits between the store and the null-out; confirm the fs_context teardown frees param->string exactly when it is non-NULL.

findings.title: Ordering invariant: validation and duplicate checks must precede the string steal
findings.anchor: if (ctx->name) return invalfc(...); ctx->name = param->string; (mirrors Opt_release_agent)
findings.bug_class: State-ordering hazard enabling leak or use-after-free
findings.invariant: All value validation (empty/length/charset) and the duplicate-set guard must run and possibly early-return BEFORE param->string is stolen, so no path both takes ownership and then returns an error or overwrites a prior owner.
findings.failure_mode: If the duplicate guard or any validation were reordered below ctx->name = param->string, a second name= token would overwrite ctx->name and leak the first buffer, or an error return after the steal would strand the layer to free a string ctx still references (UAF/double-free). As shown the order is correct; the risk is a future edit that moves the assignment up.
findings.what_to_confirm: That the respecified check and every invalfc early-return in the Opt_name and Opt_release_agent cases lexically precede the param->string steal.

findings.title: Name length is the sole guard for a downstream fixed-size buffer
findings.anchor: if (param->size > MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN - 1) return invalfc(fc, "Name too long");
findings.code:
if (param->size > MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN - 1)
			return invalfc(fc, "Name too long");
findings.bug_class: Length field vs fixed buffer (downstream copy)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (downstream copy into root->name[MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN], file not provided)
findings.invariant: param->size (attacker-controlled) must be bounded to at most MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN - 1 before ctx->name is later copied into the fixed root->name[MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN] array, leaving room for the NUL.
findings.failure_mode: This function does no fixed-buffer copy itself, but the stored name is copied into root->name during hierarchy build; if this guard were removed, off-by-one, or the downstream copy used a different constant, an over-long attacker name would overflow that fixed array (CWE-787). The guard here is correct (max stored length + NUL == MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN).
findings.what_to_confirm: That the downstream copy of ctx->name into root->name uses MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN and NUL-terminates, and that no other path populates the name without this length check.

findings.title: isalnum() called on a possibly-negative signed char
findings.bug_class: ctype misuse / potential out-of-bounds table read
findings.invariant: Arguments to ctype classification functions must be representable as unsigned char (or EOF); a signed char holding a byte >= 0x80 must be cast to unsigned char before the call.
findings.failure_mode: param->string bytes are fully attacker-controlled, so any byte >= 0x80 makes c negative; passing a negative value to isalnum is undefined behaviour and, in a table-based ctype implementation, can index before the classification table (OOB read). In the kernel isalnum is a self-contained macro so real impact is negligible, but the pattern is a latent defect; the fix is isalnum((unsigned char)c).
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the isalnum used here is the table-indexed libc form or a bounded kernel macro, and add an (unsigned char) cast regardless.

findings.title: release_agent path stored without content validation
findings.bug_class: Attacker-controlled usermode-helper path captured; privilege depends on an external gate
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (release-agent capability gate and exec path, file not provided)
findings.invariant: The mount/write path that eventually honours ctx->release_agent must enforce the appropriate capability check (e.g. CAP_SYS_ADMIN in the initial namespace) so an unprivileged user cannot cause an arbitrary binary to be executed.
findings.failure_mode: Unlike Opt_name, the release_agent string is stored verbatim with no character or path validation. If the downstream capability gate is missing or bypassable (the class of bug behind CVE-2022-0492 in this same feature), an attacker who can mount cgroup v1 in a user namespace can set an arbitrary release_agent that runs with host privilege on release notification. This file only captures the value; it is flagged so the reviewer confirms the external gate.
findings.what_to_confirm: That honouring ctx->release_agent is gated by a capability check in the initial user namespace before the usermode helper is spawned.

audit_checklist: Confirm every store of param->string (fc->source line 16, ctx->release_agent line 58, ctx->name line 82) is immediately followed by param->string = NULL with no intervening error return.
Confirm the fs_context teardown (put_fs_context / vfs_parse_fs_param) frees param->string only when non-NULL, so a stolen string is never double-freed.
Confirm no code path takes ownership of param->string and then returns an error before the null-out (ordering of respecified/validation checks in Opt_name and Opt_release_agent).
Confirm the downstream copy of ctx->name into root->name[MAX_CGROUP_ROOT_NAMELEN] uses the same constant, respects the length checked here, and NUL-terminates.
Confirm param->size accurately reflects the readable length of param->string (fs_context layer contract) so the character-validation loop cannot read out of bounds.
Add an (unsigned char) cast to the isalnum() argument at line 73 to avoid signed-char UB.
Confirm an appropriate capability gate protects the release_agent feature before the stored path is executed (cf. CVE-2022-0492).
Confirm ctx->subsys_mask bit index i (1 << i) can never reach the width of int given CGROUP_SUBSYS_COUNT.
